items.q: What did Rutherford's alpha-scattering experiment show about the atom?
items.correct: Most of the atom is empty space, with a tiny dense positive nucleus.
items.misconception: It showed that atoms are solid spheres of positive charge with electrons embedded inside.
items.random_wrong: It showed that alpha particles can pass through any material because they are massless.
items.why: Students confuse the plum-pudding model (which Rutherford disproved) with the conclusion of his experiment.

items.q: Which type of nuclear radiation is most ionising?
items.correct: Alpha radiation — large, doubly charged particles cause the most ionisation per unit distance.
items.misconception: Gamma is most ionising because it can travel furthest and pass through almost anything.
items.random_wrong: Beta radiation is most ionising because it consists of fast electrons.
items.why: Students conflate penetrating power with ionising power — gamma penetrates most because it ionises least, the opposite of intuition.

items.q: After one half-life, what happens to an individual radioactive nucleus?
items.correct: It has a 50 percent chance of having decayed — radioactive decay is random for any single nucleus.
items.misconception: Every nucleus is guaranteed to have half-decayed after one half-life.
items.random_wrong: The nucleus shrinks to half its original size after one half-life.
items.why: Students treat half-life as deterministic for individual atoms, when it is a statistical statement about large populations of nuclei.

items.q: Alpha emission changes a nucleus by:
items.why: It's mass -4, atomic -2. “mass -1, atomic 0” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Half-life of 8 hours; after 24 hours, fraction left:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1/8. “1/2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Activity 800 Bq. Half-life 5 min. Activity (Bq) after 15 min?
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
